questions.stem: Which of the following best describes nonmaterial culture?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The intangible ideas, values, beliefs, and norms shared within a society.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cultural change that results when two cultures come into continuous firsthand contact.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The perception of being worse off compared with the others one uses as a reference.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A condition of normlessness in which social norms are weak, unclear, or conflicting.

questions.explanation: Nonmaterial culture is best described as the intangible ideas, values, beliefs, and norms shared within a society. The other options describe different concepts.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: Nonmaterial culture is one of the two main components of culture. What is the component it is contrasted with?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Material culture: the physical objects and artifacts a society produces

choices.label: B
choices.text: Popular culture: the widespread tastes of the general public

choices.label: C
choices.text: High culture: the refined tastes of a society's elite

choices.label: D
choices.text: Counterculture: a group that rejects mainstream values

hints.rationale: Culture is split into material (physical objects) and nonmaterial (ideas and norms) components.

hints.prompt: Which of the following would count as part of nonmaterial culture?

choices.label: B

choices.label: C
choices.text: The highways a society builds

choices.label: D
choices.text: The clothing a society sews

hints.rationale: Nonmaterial culture consists of intangible ideas and values, not physical objects.

hints.prompt: One option is a distractor that actually describes anomie. Which option is it?

choices.label: A

choices.label: B

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of assimilation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Immigrant families adopting the dominant language and customs over generations until differences fade.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The smartphones, cars, and clothing produced and used within a society.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A formerly incarcerated person being treated first and foremost as an ex-convict.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Attending the opera and formal galas associated with a society's upper class.

questions.explanation: An example of assimilation is: immigrant families adopting the dominant language and customs over generations until differences fade. In general, assimilation refers to the process by which a minority group gradually takes on the culture of the dominant group. The other options illustrate different concepts.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: Assimilation refers to which process?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A minority group gradually taking on the culture of the dominant group

choices.label: B
choices.text: Two groups keeping entirely separate cultures side by side

choices.label: C
choices.text: The physical objects a society produces and uses

choices.label: D
choices.text: A negative label that overtakes a person's identity

hints.rationale: Assimilation is the process by which a minority group gradually adopts the dominant culture.

hints.prompt: How does assimilation differ from multiculturalism?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Assimilation ends in blended sameness, while multiculturalism preserves distinct cultures

choices.label: B
choices.text: Assimilation preserves distinct cultures, while multiculturalism erases them

choices.label: C
choices.text: They are two words for the same process

choices.label: D
choices.text: Both refer only to the physical goods a culture makes

hints.rationale: Assimilation moves toward one shared culture, whereas multiculturalism maintains separate identities.

hints.prompt: One option is a distractor that illustrates a master status (being seen first as an ex-convict). Which option is it?

choices.label: B

choices.label: C
choices.text: A formerly incarcerated person treated first and foremost as an ex-convict

choices.label: D
choices.text: Attending opera and galas linked to the upper class

hints.rationale: Being defined primarily by one label reflects a master status/stigma, not assimilation.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of differential association?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The same coffee chain and phone brand appearing in cities on every continent.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Wearing very casual clothing to a formal dinner and drawing disapproving looks.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A youth learning shoplifting methods and attitudes from a delinquent peer group.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A large coordinated campaign pressing lawmakers for new environmental protections.

questions.explanation: An example of differential association is: a youth learning shoplifting methods and attitudes from a delinquent peer group. In general, differential association refers to the theory that deviance is learned through interaction with others who model and reward it. The other options illustrate different concepts.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: Differential association theory explains deviance as mainly the result of what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Learning deviant behavior and attitudes through interaction with others

choices.label: B
choices.text: Inherited biological traits present from birth

choices.label: C
choices.text: A society-wide breakdown of norms

choices.label: D
choices.text: Random individual choice unrelated to others

hints.rationale: Differential association holds that deviance is learned through interaction with others who model and reward it.

hints.prompt: How does differential association differ from strain theory?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It emphasizes learning deviance from others, not blocked access to goals

choices.label: B
choices.text: It emphasizes blocked access to goals, not learning from others

choices.label: C
choices.text: Both claim deviance is purely genetic

choices.label: D
choices.text: Neither involves other people

hints.rationale: Differential association centers on social learning, whereas strain theory centers on blocked goals.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of globalization?

choices.label: D

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of allostatic load?

choices.text: The long-term bodily toll of years of unrelieved stress.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Concluding one is afraid because one notices a racing heart and trembling.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Making a study schedule to tackle the stress of an upcoming exam.
